segments.s228-g113: خروشان is a participial/adverbial form meaning 'roaring' and modifies the action that follows. سوی میمنه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the right' (سوی + میمنه) with the direction word میمنه ('right side') after سوی. راه جست is a compact verbal phrase where راه (here 'way' or 'on the path') precedes the past tense/singular perfect جست 'he leapt/ sprang' — classical word order where the verb appears at the end.
segments.s228-g114: ز لشکر is an ablative prepositional phrase 'from the host/army' using poetic ز for 'from'. سوی کندر آمد is 'came toward Kondar' with سوی+place and آمد (past tense 'came') as the main verb; نخست follows as an adverb 'first' modifying آمد (i.e. 'he came first'). The sequence places the verb toward the end, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s228-g115: همه (a universal quantifier 'all' or 'the whole') precedes میمنه ('the right [side/wing]'). پاک here functions adverbially/intensifying ('completely'); together they form the subject/affected noun phrase. بر هم درید is a verbal phrase: درید 'tore/rent' is the past tense verb, and بر هم indicates mutual/reciprocal action ('upon one another' / 'into pieces among themselves'), so the clause means that the entire right was cleanly/completely torn apart.
segments.s228-g116: This clause lists the consequences: بسی is an intensifier 'many/much' (classical use), followed by objects ترگ و سر بد 'shreds and badly (mangled) heads' (ترگ 'shred/rag', سر 'head', بد 'bad/ill' used adverbially/qualifying the state of the heads). که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/so (the result) was that' and تن را ندید is a negated past verb construction where را marks تن ('the body') as definite object and ندید is 'did not see' — i.e. 'so many rags and mangled heads that the body could not be seen.'
segments.s228-g57: A coordinated nominal phrase: چنان (a determiner/adverb here meaning “such”) modifies the following list of nouns. The nouns are linked by repeated و (the coordinating conjunction “and”), yielding the catalogue “نیزه و خنجر و گرز و تیر” (spears, daggers, maces, and arrows). Word order is the expected noun-list after a demonstrative/qualifier.
segments.s228-g58: Relative clause introduced by که (here “that/which”) modifies the preceding list. شده (شد + ساخته in classical order: ساخته 'made' + auxiliary شد 'became/was') forms a passive meaning “was made/fashioned.” بر takes the force “against” here, and یل شیرگیر is the head noun + epithet (‘the hero who catches lions’). Note the archaic ordering 'که شد ساخته' instead of the modern 'که ساخته شد'.
segments.s228-g59: گمان برد is an impersonal idiom meaning “one would suppose/one might think.” کاندر = کان + در (classical fused form) = “in which/in that place”; نیستان is the locative noun “reed‑bed.” شدست is the perfective/passive auxiliary “it has become/it was.” Together they mean roughly “one would suppose that in the reed‑bed it had become (so).”
segments.s228-g60: ز is the poetic preposition “from/because of.” خون روی کشور is a genitive phrase where روی means “surface/face” (i.e., the surface of the country). میستان is used metaphorically (“wine‑house(s)/taverns”), and شدست again is “has become.” The clause says that, because of blood, the country's surface had turned into wine‑houses (a bloody‑as‑tavern metaphor).
segments.s228-g61: This clause is compressed and somewhat archaic. Grammatically, the phrase begins with بیک (an old/archaic form), followed by زخم (“wound”) and then نیزه (spear) and کردی (second‑person past ‘you did/made’). قلم (“pen”) is placed at the end as the object/complement. The construction expresses a figurative result: by (a) wound you effected/made something of the spears — the word order places the agent/measure up front and the effected object (قلم) after the verb, as is common in classical poetic compression.
segments.s228-g62: A sequence of coordinated adjectives modifying an understood plural or the preceding noun: خروشان and جوشان are participial/adjectival forms meaning “roaring” and “boiling/seething,” linked by و, followed by دشمن (enemy) modified by the postpositive adjective دژم (“sullen/grim”). Adjectives follow the noun in Persian and are coordinated with و.
segments.s228-g63: دلیران ایران is a genitive phrase “the brave ones of Iran.” پس پشت اوی is a fused classical locative: پس‌پشت = “rear/behind,” and اوی is the possessive/pronominal ending “his.” Thus the phrase means “(the brave men of Iran) at his rear/behind him.”
segments.s228-g64: بکینه is an adverbial/prepositional phrase “with hatred” or “in revenge,” followed by دل آگنده (“heart filled”) where آگنده is a postpositive adjective meaning “filled/overflowing.” و links the coordination to جنگ جوی, a compound meaning “war‑seeking” or “eager for battle” (جوی here functioning as ‘seeker/‑seeking’). Overall: their hearts were full of hatred and they sought battle.
segments.s228-g81: چنین گفت رستم بایرانیان is a clipped classical word order: manner (چنین, “thus”) + verb (گفت, “said”) + subject (رستم, “Rostam”) + indirect object/recipient (بایرانیان, “to the Iranians”). In modern Persian you would expect subject before verb, but older/poetic order often places the verb earlier. بایرانیان is the dative/benefactive recipient (“to/for the Iranians”).
segments.s228-g82: کزین جنگ دشمن کند جان زیان is a subordinate clause introduced by کزین (ک + ز + این = “from this / because of this”), followed by the noun جنگ (“battle/war”). دشمن is the grammatical subject, کند is the (classical/poetic) present/simple verb ‘does/causes’ (here future/inevitability is implied by context), and جان زیان is an accusative noun phrase meaning “loss/harm to life” (جان = life, زیان = loss). Word order is subject–verb–object in a terse poetic phrasing.
segments.s228-g83: هم‌اکنون ز پیلان و از خواسته begins with هم‌اکنون (“right now/at once”). ز (poetic form of از) marks origin or cause: “from / because of.” پیلان (plural of پیل) = “elephants.” و + از joins another source phrase, از خواسته here means “from the booty/what is desired/what has been taken” — a succinct poetic phrase indicating sources of what follows. The whole segment lists the resources by which something is furnished.
segments.s228-g84: همان تخت و آن تاج آراسته is a nominal predicate: همان تخت (“that very throne”) و آن تاج (“and that crown”) followed by آراسته, an adjectival/predicate form meaning “adorned / prepared / made splendid.” In Persian poetry the adjective can serve as the main predicative element; the previous segment supplies the means (from elephants and spoils) by which the throne and crown become adorned.
segments.s228-g85: ستانم ز چینی بایران is a compact first‑person clause. ستانم is the classical 1st‑person present/subjunctive form of ستاندن, here idiomatically “I will take/seize/levy.” ز چینی = “from the Chinese / from Chinese (things)” (چینی as source/possession). بایران (orthography here runs together) functions as the beneficiary phrase “to the Iranians” (give/assign to Iran/Iranians) — the verb ستانم sets up a take–give pair in this line.
segments.s228-g86: بدان شادمان روز فرخ نهم parses as بد + آن (contracted here as بدان “to those/for that”), شادمان used as a plural/collective noun “the rejoicing/happy ones,” روز فرخ “the auspicious/fortunate day,” and نهم (from نهادن) “I place / I give / I set.” Together it means “I will give/assign [it] to those rejoicing on that fortunate day.” Note the archaic contraction and the use of an adjective as a noun (شادمان) typical of epic diction.
segments.s228-g87: نباشد جز ایرانیان شاد کس is an optative/negative construction: نباشد is a poetic jussive/negative present (“let there not be / there shall not be”), جز marks exception (“except”), ایرانیان is the group being excepted, شاد modifies کس (شاد کس = “a happy person”). The whole means “let there be no happy person except the Iranians” (i.e., only Iranians shall be joyful).
segments.s228-g88: پی رخش و ایزد مرا یار بس contains two allies listed: پی رخش (literally “(at) the track/of Rakhsh” but here idiomatically “by/with my steed Rakhsh”) و ایزد (“and Providence / God”). مرا = “to me” (accusative), یار = “friend/ally,” and بس is an old/sentential predicate meaning “is enough / suffices.” Put together: “Rakhsh and Providence are (will be) sufficient allies for me.” The clause is elliptical and typical of heroic address.
segments.s228-g17: The segment begins with the prepositional phrase 'بر شنگل' (بر + noun) meaning 'upon/on Shangal', followed by the past tense verb 'آمد' ('he came'). The phrase 'به آواز گفت' uses به to mark manner ('in/by a voice') and گفت as the third-person past of 'say' — so literally 'he came upon Shangal and said aloud.' Word order is prepositional phrase → verb → manner phrase.
segments.s228-g18: This is a vocative/exclamatory address introduced as one unit: 'که‌ای' functions like an old-fashioned interjection/vocative ('O' or 'that O'), followed by two stacked pejorative adjectives 'بدنژاد فرومایه' ('ill-/low-born, base') modifying the noun 'جفت' ('pair' or couple) used here as an insult. Adjectives precede the noun for emphasis; the whole phrase is what was said aloud.
segments.s228-g19: Syntactically this is object + predicate + verb + postposed subject: 'مرا' (me; object, written here as م + را) takes the object marker را, 'نام رستم' is the name phrase ('the name Rostam'), and 'کند' is a present/habitual third-person verb 'makes/calls'. The agent 'زال زر' ('Zal the Golden') follows the verb in typical Persian poetic word order. Note the tokenization: 'م' + 'را' form the single object pronoun 'مرا'.
segments.s228-g20: This is a second utterance directed at the addressee: 'تو' (you) + epithet 'سگزی' ('dog‑born'/'doglike') followed by the question word 'چرا' ('why') and the present-tense verb 'خوانی' (2nd/3rd-present form of خواندن here used in the sense 'to call/summon'). 'ای بدگهر' is a vocative ('O evil‑minded one'). Word order is pronoun → insult → question → verb → vocative.
segments.s228-g21: 'نگه کن' is an imperative: نگه (a noun/verb root 'look/gaze' used imperatively) + کن ('do' as imperative), so 'look!' or 'take heed!'. 'که' introduces the content clause 'that/for', and the rest states the proposition: 'سگزی کنون مرگ تست' — 'you dog-born, now death is yours.' Here 'کنون' is temporal 'now' and the clause uses compressed Old/poetic possessive or copular forms (see notes on تست below).
segments.s228-g22: This clause continues the assertion: 'کفن بی‌گمان جوشن و ترگ تست' — 'your shroud, without doubt, (is) armor and your rag (is) a cuirass.' The nouns 'کفن' and 'جوشن' are in contrast/metaphor (shroud vs. armor). 'بی‌گمان' is an adverb 'undoubtedly'. The final 'تست' again stands in the older/poetic line as a copular/possessive form ('is yours' or 'yours') — the copula/possessive is elliptical in heroic verse.
segments.s228-g23: 'همی گشت' is a frequentative/continuous past narrative construction: همی indicates ongoing action and گشت is past/frequentative 'went/kept going' or 'was turning/roaming'. 'با او' ('with him') is the accompanying phrase, and 'به آوردگاه' ('to the battlefield') is the goal. Word order is adverbial/continuous marker → verb → companion → goal.
segments.s228-g24: This nominal phrase describes location/setting: 'میان دو صف' ('between two rows/lines') modifies 'برکشیده سپاه' — 'the upraised/arrayed host'. 'برکشیده' is a participial adjective meaning 'upraised/arrayed' (the army drawn up in formation). The whole phrase is a post-nominal modifier chain typical of Persian epic diction.
segments.s228-g25: یکی نیزه زد برگرفتش ز زین is a compressed sequence of actions. یکی (subject) here means 'someone'; نیزه زد is a simple past verb phrase 'thrust/struck with a spear'. برگرفتش is برگرفت + ـش (perfect-past stem with the third-person object clitic) = 'took him up / seized him'. ز زین uses the poetic preposition ز 'from' + زین 'saddle' = 'from the saddle'. The clauses are chained without an explicit subject repeated: the same actor performs each verb.
segments.s228-g26: نگونسار کرد و بزد بر زمین contains two coordinated predicates. نگونسار کرد uses نگونسار (an adjective 'mournful/woebegone') plus کرد as a light/causative verb 'made [him] mournful' (کرد here causative/performative). و links the clauses. بزد is the past 'he struck/ smote' and بر زمین 'upon the ground' is the prepositional/locative complement; Persian order places the verb before its locative complement in poetry, but meaning is 'struck him to the ground.'
segments.s228-g27: برو بر گذر کرد is a verbal sequence where ب��و (go/advance) combines with the light verb کرد to form a past action 'he went/advanced onto the pass'—بر گذر = 'onto the pass/passage'. The subject is omitted (carried over from context). و او را نخست places او را (definite object 'him') with the adverb نخست 'first', marking that the following action happens to him first.
segments.s228-g28: بشمشیر برد is an instrumental phrase: بشمشیر 'by/with the sword' + برد (here the resultive/past 'took/won/overpowered by the sword') = 'he felled/overcame him with the sword'. آنگاهی 'then' introduces the next action; شیر دست is a compound epithet 'lion‑hand(ed)' (an epithet or nickname meaning the brave or lion‑hand), likely the agent of what follows in the next line.
segments.s228-g29: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/they withdrew'. زان روی is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'for that reason' / 'thereupon' (زان = 'from that/therefore', روی here forming the idiom). کنداوران is a plural noun (here likely a group name or epithet) standing in apposition as part of the list of those who moved off.
segments.s228-g30: This segment lists names/epithets: بزهر is a proper name; آب‌داده is a compound adjective 'water‑given / water‑nurtured' attached to that name; پرندآوران is another plural epithet 'bird‑bringers' or 'the falconers/winged‑ones'. Grammatically these are appositive nouns/adjectives continuing the list introduced by the previous clause.
segments.s228-g31: چو is the conjunction 'when'; شنگل is a proper name (here the subject). گریزان شد is a periphrastic expression: گریزان (fleeing) + شد (became) = 'fled' / 'became in flight'. از پیلتن is a prepositional phrase 'from Pilten' (place from which he fled).
segments.s228-g32: پراگنده گشتند is 'were scattered' (پراگنده 'scattered' + گشتند 'they became/were'), a typical past-resultive construction meaning the group dispersed. زان انجمن 'that assembly' (زان = 'from that/thereupon' functioning adverbially here, انجمن = 'assembly') completes the thought: 'that assembly was scattered.'
segments.s228-g73: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for'. با او بجنگ is an infinitival/agent phrase 'to fight with him' (ب- prefix + جنگ), with verb before adverbial as is common in poetry. اندرون پای نیست is a negated nominal clause literally 'inside there is no foot' — پای is used metaphorically as 'standing/one who can stand/oppose' and نیست is the negative copula 'does not exist'.
segments.s228-g74: چنو (= چنان) is a demonstrative/adverb 'such/so' introducing the generalizing statement. در جهان is the locative 'in the world'. لشکر آرای is a poetic compound: لشکر 'army' + آرای (here acting as a modifier/agent) meaning one who arrays or outfits an army; نیست negates the whole phrase 'there is no ...'.
segments.s228-g75: کسی کو is an archaic relative/indefinite 'whoever' (کو used as relative). کند is the present/ habitual verb 'does/makes' (here 'treats as / turns into'). زین = از این 'of/from this' (orthographic fused form) and سخن داستان is the object phrase 'a story from these words' (داستان as 'tale').
segments.s228-g76: نباشد is a fixed negative copular form used impersonally/potentially ('there would not be' / 'is not to be found'); خردمند is the subject 'wise person'; همداستان is a predicate adjective 'of the same mind / in agreement'. Together: 'no wise person will be of the same mind.'
segments.s228-g77: که is the connective 'that'. پرخاشخر here functions as a proper/name or epithet (a quarrelsome/aggressive person); نامور modifies it as 'famed/renowned'. The following numeral phrase صد هزار quantifies the host attributed to him.
segments.s228-g78: بسنده نبودند is a negated predication 'were not sufficient' (بسنده 'enough/sufficient', نبودند past/plural of بودن 'to be' meaning 'were not'). با یک سوار is an adjunct 'against a single rider/horseman' — the whole clause says even that great force would not suffice against one mounted man.
segments.s228-g79: ازین (= از این) is the fused prepositional phrase 'from this / because of this' (ین here the orthographic element). کین 'grudge/enmity' is the subject of the event, بد آمد is a light-verb phrase 'came ill' = 'turned out badly / befell (someone) badly'. بافراسیاب 'with Afrasiab' indicates the affected party ('it went badly for Afrasiab').
segments.s228-g80: ز رستم 'because of/from Rostam' gives the cause. کجا یابد is an interrogative inversion 'where can he find' (کجا + present/subjunctive-style یابد 'finds/attains'). آرام و خواب are the coordinated nouns 'peace (rest) and sleep' — a rhetorical question about Afrasiab's inability to rest because of Rostam.
segments.s228-g41: This is a noun phrase in fronted order: 'بتنها' (alone) qualifies 'کسی' (anyone). 'رزم' (battle) is the direct object of the implied verb in the next segment, and 'با اژدها' is a prepositional phrase 'with the dragon' (با + noun). In older/poetic Persian the adjective/quantifier 'بتنها' appears before the noun for emphasis.
segments.s228-g42: This clause contains a negative jussive/optative and a conditional: 'نجوید' is ن + جوید, here functioning as an injunction/negative 'let him not seek' (negative prefix ن on the present form). 'چو' = 'if' introduces the condition; 'جوید' is the positive present/subjunctive 'he seeks'; 'نیابد' is ن + یابد 'he does not find' (future/optative sense in classical usage); 'رها' is a noun meaning 'release/freedom' (object of 'نیابد'). The verbs follow the classical pattern where negative prefix appears directly on the present form.
segments.s228-g43: 'بدو' is the dative/clitic phrase 'to him' placed before the verb for emphasis. 'گفت' is the past verbal root 'said' (here used as the speech verb introducing direct speech). 'خاقان' is the subject 'the khaghan' (title). The sequence 'ت' + 'را' represent the pronoun 'ترا' (to you) split into enclitic 'ت' and the object marker 'را' — a common poetic splitting; 'بامداد' 'at dawn/morning' gives the time when the addressed matter applies.
segments.s228-g44: A parallel copular clause: 'دگر بود' = 'there was another/was different'. 'رای' (counsel/plan) is the first predicate, then 'و' joins the second parallel member 'دگر بود یاد' where 'یاد' functions as 'thought/intent/memory'. The verb 'بود' is the past/copular verb (classical past tense) used twice to state two different dispositions.
segments.s228-g45: 'سپه را' marks the army as the direct object (را after the noun for emphasis), and 'بفرمود' is an honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered'. 'تا' introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'). 'همگروه' here functions adverbially — 'as a group' / 'together' — indicating how the action should be carried out.
segments.s228-g46: 'برانند' is the subjunctive/pluperfective form of راندن used after 'تا' to express the intended action ('that they should drive/drive away'). 'یکسر' is an adverb meaning 'all at once' or 'entirely' modifying the verb. 'بکردار' appears as an attributive/qualifying element modifying the manner or group (an archaic/poetic form here); 'کوه' is the locative target 'mountain' (object/place related to the driving). Together the phrase means they are to drive them in one movement with regard to the mountain.
segments.s228-g47: 'سرافراز' is a definite noun meaning 'the exalted/honored one' and is marked by 'را' as definite object. 'در میان' is a prepositional phrase 'into the midst' or 'among them'. 'آورند' is the present/pluperfective verb 'they brought/they caused to be brought', so the clause literally says 'they brought the honored one into the middle'.
segments.s228-g48: Here 'تنومند را' is the affected person marked by 'را'. 'جان زیان آورند' is a verbal phrase in which 'زیان آوردن' (to cause harm) takes 'جان' (life) as its object: literally 'they brought harm to the life (of the stout one)', i.e. they grievously wounded or endangered his life. The verb 'آورند' again is the past/plural form 'they brought/caused'.
segments.s228-g9: چنین (an adverb) precedes the verb گفت (past 'said') for a classical/poetic word order; رستم is the subject placed after the verb (verb–subject order is common in older Persian). که introduces the reported content clause ('that'), and از کردگار begins a prepositional phrase ('from the Creator') which supplies the source of what he sought.
segments.s228-g10: نجستم is a 1st‑person past form meaning 'I sought' (classical inflection of جستن). جزین ('except/other than this') modifies آرزوی آشکار ('this manifest/clear wish'), so the verb's object is 'nothing but this clear desire'. Word order puts verb before its noun phrase object, as typical in this poetic register.
segments.s228-g11: که again introduces the subordinate clause continuing the reported speech. بیگانه‌ای is an indefinite subject ('a stranger'); زان is an archaising prepositional form equivalent to از آن ('from that') and links to بزرگ انجمن ('the great assembly/council'). The phrase names who might act.
segments.s228-g12: دلیری کند is a verb phrase where کند ('does/makes') is third‑person and makes دلیری ('bravery/daring') into a verbal action ('shows daring' or 'dares'); رزم جوید is a second verb phrase in parallel where جوید ('seeks') takes رزم ('battle/combat') as its object. ز من ('from me' / 'against me') follows the verb, a common poetic inversion (i.e. 'seeks battle with me').
segments.s228-g13: The paired negator نه ... نه ('neither ... nor') begins the list of things that 'will not remain'. ماند is the verb 'remains/is left' in third person; از ایشان ('of/from them') follows the verb to indicate possession/source. هند appears as another negated item in the same series. (سقلاب is an obscure/archaic noun here.)
segments.s228-g14: The negation pattern continues with نه introducing further items. شمشیر هندی is a noun + adjective combination ('Indian sword' — adjective follows the noun), and چینی پرند is another nominal phrase ('Chinese bird(s)' / 'Chinese winged things'); the items are juxtaposed as things that will not remain.
segments.s228-g15: پی و بیخ ('pi va bīkh') is an idiomatic pair meaning 'root and base' or 'root and branch' (here 'their roots/foundations'). ایشان ('their/them') marks whose roots these are. نمانم is 1st‑person negative ('I will not leave / I will not let remain') used in the narrative to express determination; بجای ('in place' / 'standing') completes the sense 'will not leave ... standing'.
segments.s228-g16: The clause repeats نمانم ('I will not leave') and bترکان is a contracted written form of به ترکان ('to/for the Turks') — a common classical cliticization where the preposition به joins the following noun. The coordinated list سر و دست و پای ('head and hand and foot') are the concrete things he says he will not leave to the Turks; objects follow the verb as in the previous clause.
segments.s228-g65: ز بس introduces a source/causal sense: ز + بس = 'from/owing to so many'. The list نیزه و گرز و گوپال و تیغ is a simple coordinated noun phrase ('spears and maces and Gopāl and swords') where each noun is linked by و; word order is noun-conjunction-noun as normal in Persian enumerations.
segments.s228-g66: تو گفتی is the verbal clause with an explicit 2nd-person subject 'you' (تو) plus the perfective/definite form گفتی 'you said'; همی is an older/poetic particle meaning 'continually/still' and modifies the following verb phrase; ژاله بارد ز میغ is a verbless verblike clause where بارَد (here in the sense of 'falls/rains' — archaic form of باریدن) governs ژاله 'drops/dew' and ز میغ 'from the blade' marks source. The whole clause reports speech: 'you said (that) drops keep falling from the blade.'
segments.s228-g67: ز before a noun phrase signals 'out of/from' or 'as for' — here marking material coming from the slain; کشته is used as a substantive 'the slain' (not an active participle), همه 'all' intensifies/quantifies, and دشت آوردگاه is a compound where دشت (plain) is specified by آوردگاه 'the place of bringing' = 'battlefield plain'.
segments.s228-g68: A series of nouns joined by و (تن و پشت و سر) lists body-parts 'bodies, backs, heads'; بود is the past copula 'was/there were' asserting existence/appearance, and the following و ترگ و کلاه continues the list with inanimate items 'rags and helmets'. The conjunctions simply connect items in the enumerative predicate governed by بود.
segments.s228-g69: This is a continuation of an enumerative origin-list introduced by ز/از. ز چینی و شگنی opens with 'from the Chinese and Shagani'; شگنی is an archaic/obscure ethnonym or adjective and is left here as a nominal element. از هندوی repeats the preposition (از) to mark another origin 'from the Indian(s)'. Repetition of ز/از is a poetic device to list sources.
segments.s228-g70: Again the preposition ز introduces further origins: ز سقلاب و هری و از پهلوی = 'from Saklāb and Harī and from Pahlav(i)'. Here سقلاب is an obscure place/name, هری refers to Harī/Harirud or the Harē region, and از پهلوی repeats 'from Pahlav' (Pahlavi/Pahlavian area). The alternating ز/از is common in poetic lists.
segments.s228-g71: سپه بود is the copular clause 'the army/host was'; چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and خاک در پای کوه is the simile's object 'dust at the foot/base of the mountain'. Word order is standard poetic inversion but reads literally 'the host was like dust in/at the foot of the mountain.'
segments.s228-g72: ز marks an agent or cause 'by/from'; یک مرد is 'one man' (here 'a single man' or 'one valiant man'), سگزی is an adjective or epithet attached to مرد (the exact historic/poetic sense is obscure in the lexicon given) and شده is the past participle 'made/become' used here in a passive/result sense; همگروه (همگروه) means 'in one group/companioned' — together the phrase says roughly 'made/assembled into one company by a single (sagazy) man.'
segments.s228-g89: یکی را introduces an individual as an emphasized/definite object (را marks it); here it functions like 'not one (of them)'. ز before the following list is the poetic preposition 'from/of' that makes the list partitive. شگنان و سقلاب و چین is a coordinated list (و = 'and') of three items; چین here is the noun 'wrinkle/fold' rather than the country.
segments.s228-g90: نمانم is first-person singular with the negative prefix n-: here 'I will not leave' / 'I will not let remain' (i.e. 'I will leave none'). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'who'). پی here is the fossilized poetic form for 'پا/پای' meaning 'foot'; برنهد is a present/subjunctive-like third-person singular compound verb meaning 'sets/places (upon)'; بر + زمین = 'on the ground'. Together پی برنهد بر زمین = 'sets his foot on the ground'.
segments.s228-g91: که at the start of this sentence links back to the previous clause with a causal/declaring 'for/that'. امروز پیروزی روز ماست is a verbless nominal clause closed by ست 'is' (classical copula): 'today (is) the day of our victory' — روز is 'day' and ماست = 'is ours/our ...'.
segments.s228-g92: بلند آسمان is an adjective + noun ('the lofty/heavenly sky'); the clause then predicates about that sky: لشکر افروز is a compound in which افروز (= 'illuminating' / 'glorifying') qualifies the role with لشکر 'host/army' (i.e. 'heaven that lights/uplifts the host'). ماست at the end is the copula again: 'the lofty heaven is the one that brightens our host.'
segments.s228-g93: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a temporal/particle (poetic) meaning roughly 'now/at this moment' and modifies the condition. نیرو دهد is a verb phrase where نیرو is the object 'strength/power' and دهد ('gives') is third-person singular; دادگر ('the Just One') is the agent — a nominative/absolute noun labeling who gives (often God or the just ruler).
segments.s228-g94: پدید آورد is a two-word verbal idea: پدید 'appear/emerge' with آورد 'brings/causes' — together 'brings forth / causes to appear'. رخش is the direct object (the famed steed Rakhsh), and رخشان هنر is an epithet: رخشان ('radiant') modifies رخش, and هنر ('prowess/virtue') is an attributive noun — 'Rakhsh, radiant in prowess.' Word order places the verb phrase before its object, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s228-g95: برین دشت is the prepositional phrase 'on this plain' (بر = 'on', ین = demonstrative 'this' fused orthographically). من is explicit first-person subject 'I'. گورستانی is the object 'a cemetery' (used as an accusative/thing-made), and کنم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive-like form of 'to make/do' — 'I will make.'
segments.s228-g96: برومند را uses را as the definite/object marker: here برومند is a person or epithet ('the noble / Barūmand') marked as the object. شارستانی is a derived noun/adjective relating to the city ('urban' / 'townsman'), and کنم again is 'I will make' — so the structure is 'I will make X شارستانی' (i.e. 'I will make/settle X into a city/townsman').
segments.s228-g105: از انبوه ایشان is an ablative/causal phrase 'on account of / from their multitude' placed before the verb for emphasis. مدارید here is an archaic verbal form; the clause pairs مدارید + باک (literally 'have fear') and functions idiomatically as an admonition 'do not fear' (negative imperative/optative sense implied in this poetic context). Word order is somewhat inverted for force: prepositional phrase → verb → noun-object (باک).
segments.s228-g106: ز دریا is a poetic locative/ablative 'from the (figurative) sea' (here describing a mass/multitude like a sea). بابر is an ambiguous lexical item in this line (likely a poetic name or an imageable noun), and it stands before the verb; اندر آرید is a compound-like sequence where اندر (into/in) precedes آرید (a poetic 3rd‑person verb 'brought/raised'). خاک is the direct object 'dust/earth' that was raised. Overall the clause reads 'from the sea (they) brought up dust'.
segments.s228-g107: همه 'everyone/all' is the subject/topic. دیده بر مغفر من نهید is literally 'placed eyes upon my maghfar' — نهید is the verbal form 'they set/placed' (past), مغفر من is a possessive phrase 'my maghfar' (maghfar here = covering/visor/helmet rather than 'forgiveness'). The preposition بر governs the noun phrase (بر + object). Word order: subject/topic → object phrase → verb.
segments.s228-g108: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. من is the subject 'I'. بر خروشم places the action 'on/into my roar' (خروش + possessive ـم 'my roar/battle‑cry'). دمید is a past tense verb 'blew'/'sound(ed)' (used poetically for sounding a cry or trumpet). و links to دهید, where دهید here is past plural 'they gave/uttered' and contextually means 'they gave a shout' — so the clause means 'when I blew (my) roar and they shouted'.
segments.s228-g109: بدرید is a past tense verb 'they tore' (from دریدن). صفهای is the plural construct 'rows/lines' (here: battle‑ranks). سقلاب و چین appear as coordinated nouns — likely place or people names (proper nouns) in this martial context — and follow the object (the lines belong to those peoples/units). The clause thus says 'they tore the ranks of Soghlāb and Chin.'
segments.s228-g110: This is an impersonal/optative construction: نباید 'it should not / must not' is followed by a clausal complement introduced by که. بیند is the present/subjunctive form 'see' (here rendered past/impersonal in English). هوا را is the definite object marker (را) attached to هوا 'the air/sky', and زمین is the logical subject 'the earth/ground'. Literal order (verb-final feel) yields 'It should not be that the earth sees the air' — poetically: 'the earth could not see the sky' (i.e., dust/smoke blocked sight).
segments.s228-g111: وزان is a fused prepositional phrase 'and/from that' pointing back to the prior position; جایگه is 'place/post/position' and functions as the locative complement to رفت, which is the past tense 'went/advanced'. چون is the simile marker 'like/as', پیل 'elephant' and مست 'drunk/intoxicated (figurative)' form the simile 'like a drunken elephant'. The verb رفت is simple past and the simile follows it for emphasis.
segments.s228-g112: یکی here is an indefinite 'one' (a certain person). گرزه is the noun 'mace/cudgel' and گاوپیکر is a descriptive compound 'cow‑bodied / ox‑like' (i.e., very large and massive) modifying the mace. به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in (his) hand' (به + noun), so the phrase describes 'one holding an ox‑bodied mace in his hand.' Word order places the descriptive adjective before the instrumental phrase.
segments.s228-g97: This is an imperative-address clause. یکی از شما (literally “one of you”) is the topical noun phrase (یکی = the numeral ‘one’ used as ‘one of’ + از شما ‘of you’). سوی لشکر (supply of direction) follows to indicate destination (‘toward the army’). شوید here is a 2nd-person plural optative/imperative form (classical use of شدن) functioning as the command verb: ‘become/go’ or simply ‘go/join’ — it issues the order to one person among the addressed group.
segments.s228-g98: This is a sequence of imperatives joined by و. بکوشید is the 2nd-person plural imperative/subjunctive of کوشیدن ‘to try/strive’ (command: ‘make an effort’). با باد is a prepositional phrase ‘with the wind’. همبر شوید uses شوید again in an optative/imperative sense ‘become’ and همبر (peer/equal) is predicate: literally ‘become equal to the wind’ — i.e. ‘be a match for the wind’ (a poetic way to urge swiftness).
segments.s228-g99: بکوبید is an imperative (2nd-person plural) from کوبیدن here meaning ‘strike/beat’ (command to sound instruments or strike). چـون introduces a temporal subordinate clause ‘when’; من بجنبم is a 1st-person present-subjunctive/indicative form (‘I move/stir/hurry’) giving the timing (‘when I move from my place’). ز جای is the prepositional phrase ‘from (my) place’ indicating the moment of motion that should trigger the striking.
segments.s228-g100: This is the coordinated instruction to other addressees: شما is the subject ‘you (pl.)’. برفرازید is an imperative (2nd-person plural) from برفرازیدن ‘to lift up / raise aloft’ (command to hold instruments high). سنج و درای are the objects (names of percussion instruments: ‘cymbals and drums’) placed after the verb in customary SOV‑like order for objects following imperative in Persian poetry.
segments.s228-g101: زمین را is the definite direct object marked with را (‘the earth’). سراسر کنید is a causative/imperative construction: سراسر (throughout/entire) + کنید (2nd-person plural imperative of کردن ‘make/do’) = ‘make [it] all/throughout’. آبنوس follows as the predicate noun/adjective specifying the result (‘ebony / black’): ‘make the earth all ebony’ (a poetic image).
segments.s228-g102: This is an address to the horsemen: بگرد is an imperative (2nd-person plural) meaning ‘turn / go about / ride around’ (here: ‘ride around’). سواران is the vocative/appellative ‘horsemen’. و links the following phrase, آوای کوس (literally ‘the voice/sound of the drum’), which is an object/goal: the clause calls them to ride and to raise/sound the war‑drum. In poetry the verb for ‘sound’ is left implicit and the noun phrase denotes the required action.
segments.s228-g103: بکوبید is again the 2nd-person plural imperative ‘strike/beat’. گوپال is here a noun/name placed as an object or accompaniment (likely a proper name or epithet) and گرز گران is a noun phrase ‘heavy mace’ (گرز = mace, گران = heavy). The sequence is an exhortation to strike with or at these things; the order is verb then objects, common in Persian poetic commands.
segments.s228-g104: چو is the comparative/simile conjunction ‘as/like’. پولاد را is the definite object marker with ‘pūlād’ (‘steel’), and پتک آهنگران is the simile’s second term: پتک ‘sledgehammer/mallet’ + آهنگران ‘blacksmiths’ (genitive relationship understood). Together the phrase reads ‘like the blacksmiths’ sledgehammer on steel’ — a simile describing the force of the blows.
segments.s228-g1: The verb بغرید (roared) appears before its subject شنگل, a common poetic inversion (verb–subject). ز پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase (ز + پیش + سپاه) meaning “in front of the host/army.” The whole segment = “the shangl roared in front of the army.”
segments.s228-g2: منم is the emphatic/self-identifying form “it is I” or “I am (here).” گفت is the past verb ‘said’ (here introducing direct speech) but its supplied sense list did not include ‘to say’ so marked below as not matched. گرداوژن is a proper name followed by the epithet رزم‌خواه (vocative/epithet), so the sequence reports speech: “’It is I,’ said Gerdaujan the warrior.”
segments.s228-g3: بگویید is an imperative/plural command “tell” (you tell). کان here functions as a demonstrative ‘that’ (classical/poetic), followed by مرد سگزی “the Sagzi man” and the question word کجاست “where is (he).” Word order is imperative + object clause: “Tell (me) where that man is.”
segments.s228-g4: This is a compact future/volitional clause. یکی is ‘one / someone,’ کرد is used in a causative/relational sense “make/appoint” (here ‘see to it that one [is sent]’), and خواهم is the first-person auxiliary indicating will/intent (“I will”). برو is an infinitival/imperative root “to go” or a command form used in the subordinate phrase, and نیزه راست “with spear upright/straight” is the manner. Overall: “I will have someone go with a straight spear.”
segments.s228-g5: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” آواز شنگل is the noun phrase “the cry/voice of the shangl.” برستم is the fused prepositional phrase (classical spelling of بر رستم) meaning “to/at Rostam” (here the target of رسید), and رسید is the past verb “reached.” The clause literally: “When the shangl’s cry reached Rostam….”
segments.s228-g6: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase “from/among the host.” نگه کرد is a periphrastic past (نگه “a look/watch” + کرد “did”) meaning “he looked / kept watch.” و او را بدید links with and + object marker را + بدید (past of دیدن) “and he saw him.” Together: “he looked from the army and saw him.”
segments.s228-g7: بدو گفت is the ditransitive sequence “(he) said to him” (بدو = to him). هان is an interjection “behold!” or “lo!”, followed by آمدم “I came / I have come.” رزمخواه is an address/epithet “O warrior.” The whole reports speech: “He said to him, ‘Behold, I have come, O warrior.’ ”
segments.s228-g8: نگر is the imperative “look!/see!” (a warning). تا here introduces the subordinate clause expressing a negative purpose/lest (“so that / lest”), followed by نگیری (present/subjunctive negative of گرفتن) “you do not take/seize.” بلشکر پناه is a compound meaning “refuge with the host/army.” The phrase as a whole = “Watch that you do not take refuge among the troops.”
segments.s228-g33: Word order is topical: numeral + noun ('دو بهره') then a genitive/partitive phrase 'از ایشان' (from/of them), then an instrumental phrase 'بشمشیر' (with a sword), and finally the verb 'کشت' (killed) in past tense. 'بشمشیر' is a prefixed instrumental (بـ + ‎شمشیر) meaning 'with the sword'. 'دو بهره' is an older/formulaic compound meaning 'two (of them)'; here it functions as the direct object (what was killed).
segments.s228-g34: Subject phrase follows: 'دلیران توران' (the brave men/warriors of Turan). The verb 'نمودند' (they showed/made) is used idiomatically with 'پشت' (back) following it: 'پشت نمودند' = 'they showed their back(s)' → 'they turned their backs' (i.e. retreated). Note the verb is 3rd person plural past.
segments.s228-g35: 'بجان شنگل' is an ablative/nominal phrase literally 'by the life of Shangal' and here functions as a circumstantial phrase indicating preservation/escape of life; 'از دست رستم' is 'out of/resting from Rostam's hand/grip'; 'بجست' is a past tense verb meaning 'leapt/escaped' (here: he leapt free). Word order places the circumstantial phrase before the verbal clause for emphasis.
segments.s228-g36: 'زره بود' is an existential/past clause 'there was armor' (or 'he wore armor'). The connector 'و' links the next clause. 'جوشن تنش را' uses the object marker 'را' after 'جوشن تنش' ('the cuirass of his body') and 'نخست' ('first') is an adverb placing that armor-piece as taken/placed on first — the verb for 'put on' is omitted but implied, so the clause reads as 'and first his cuirass (was) [on] his body.'
segments.s228-g37: 'چنین گفت شنگل' is a common poetic inversion meaning 'Shangal said thus' (چنین = thus). The subordinating particle 'که' introduces the content clause: 'این مرد نیست' — literally 'this man is not', setting up a negation or exceptional statement about that man (the complement continues in the next segment). 'گفت' is the past-tense reporting verb.
segments.s228-g38: This clause completes the negation introduced by the previous 'نیست'. 'کس' here is used negatively ('no one'), followed by the object 'او را' (him) with the adverbial 'بگیتی' ('in the world'). 'هم آورد نیست' is an idiomatic classical turn: 'هم آورد' (literally 'brings together' or 'matches') with the final 'نیست' yields 'no one in the world brings/equals him' → 'no one in the world equals him.' Word order is subject ('کس' negated), object clitic, adverbial, verb, negation.
segments.s228-g39: Poetic compression/contraction: 'پیلست' is the fused form of 'پیل است' ('is an elephant'), so the clause reads 'یکی ژنده پیل است' — 'one (a certain) ragged one is an elephant' → 'a single ragged elephant is [there]'. 'بر پشت کوه' is a locative phrase 'on the mountain's back/ridge'. Note the adjective 'ژنده' ('ragged') precedes the noun it qualifies.
segments.s228-g40: The clause begins with 'مگر' used conditionally ('unless' / 'except that'), followed by the noun 'رزم' (battle) and the verb 'سازند' (they make/prepare — 3rd person plural). 'یکسر' is an adverb 'entirely / at once' modifying the verb, and 'گروه' is the subject/object 'troop/band' (here: the whole troop). The phrase as a whole means roughly 'unless the whole troop at once prepares for battle.'
segments.s228-g49: بشمشیر is an instrumental phrase (بـ + شمشیر) meaning 'with/by sword' or 'took up the sword'. برد here is a past-tense verb placed before its subject (a common poetic order); the subject شیر follows the verb. آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time/then' modifying the whole action. The clause therefore reads: 'At that time (آن زمان) [he] with the sword (بشمشیر) took (برد) — subject: the lion (شیر)'.
segments.s228-g50: This is the completion of the action: چپ 'left' modifies دست 'hand' (i.e. left flank/left hand) and لشکر چینیان is the object marked by the accusative را omitted here (poetic compression). برشکست is a past-tense verb 'broke/shattered/overthrew' agreeing with the subject (the lion). Word order is V — (adjective) — (object phrase).
segments.s228-g51: هر آنگه که is an archaic/conjunctive temporal formula meaning 'whenever/each time that'. خنجر is the object 'dagger' and برانداختی is a past tense verb with the second-person singular ending (‑ی), 'you hurled/drew/brandished'. The clause literally: 'Whenever that you drew the dagger...'.
segments.s228-g52: همه ره = 'all (on) the road' (ره = way/road); تن = 'body'; بی‌سر = 'headless'. انداختی is the same past 2nd‑singular verb 'you threw/caused to fall'. Word order is emphatic: adverbial/quantifier (همه ره) + object (تن بی‌سر) + verb (انداختی).
segments.s228-g53: نه ... بود is a negative existential or concessive construction: نه با جنگ او 'not by his war/fighting' functions as an instrumental phrase ('by/through his battle'); کوه را پای بود literally 'the mountain had a foot' = 'the mountain had footing/standing'. بود is simple past copula 'was' (here idiomatically 'could'). The object marker را marks کوه as the definite affected thing.
segments.s228-g54: Parallel to the previous clause: نه با خشم او 'not by his anger' (instrumental), پیل را 'the elephant (as definite object)', جای بود 'had place/position' (جای = place; بود = was). The negative نه scopes over both parallel clauses, creating a parallelism 'neither ... nor ...'.
segments.s228-g55: بدان سان is an archaic compound meaning 'in that manner / so'; here it's an adverbial 'so/thus'. گرفتند is past‑tense plural 'they took/seized' and گرد اندرش = 'around him' (گرد = around; اندر + ش = in/at + his = 'his/around him'). Together: 'They so gathered/took (their positions) around him.'
segments.s228-g56: که introduces a result clause 'so that/that'. خورشید تاریک شد = 'the sun became dark' (تاریک 'dark', شد 'became'). از here has a causal sense 'because of' and برش is used elliptically to mean 'his presence/appearance' (here 'because of him'); the clause means 'that the sun was darkened because of him.'

sentences.s228-s57: Roaring, he leapt toward the right; he first came from the host toward Kondar.
sentences.s228-s58: The entire right (wing) was completely rent upon itself; many rags and mangled heads, so that the bodies were not seen.
sentences.s228-s29: Such spears, daggers, maces, and arrows as were fashioned against the lion‑catching hero.
sentences.s228-s30: One would think the reed‑bed had been changed; because of the blood, the face of the country had become a wine‑house.
sentences.s228-s31: With a single wound you turned ten spears into a pen; (you left) them roaring and seething and the enemy grim.
sentences.s228-s32: The brave men of Iran stood at his rear, their hearts filled with hatred and eager for battle.
sentences.s228-s41: Thus Rostam said to the Iranians: from this battle the enemy will cause loss of life.
sentences.s228-s42: Even now, from the elephants and from the spoils, that same throne and that crown are being (or will be) adorned.
sentences.s228-s43: I will take from the Chinese and give to the Iranians; I will present (it) to those rejoicing on that fortunate day.
sentences.s228-s44: Let no one be glad except the Iranians; Rakhsh and Providence (God) suffice as my allies.
sentences.s228-s9: He came upon (the) Shangal and, aloud, called, "O ill-born, lowly pair!"
sentences.s228-s10: Zal the Golden calls (me) by the name Rostam; why do you, dog-born, call (me so), O evil-minded one?
sentences.s228-s11: Look — for you dog-born, now death is yours; your shroud, surely, is armor and your rag a cuirass.
sentences.s228-s12: He kept moving with him to the battlefield, between the two battle-lines of the upraised host.
sentences.s228-s13: One thrust a spear, tore him from the saddle, made him woeful, and struck him to the ground.
sentences.s228-s14: He rode onto the pass and first struck him with the sword; then the lion‑hand (lit. ‘lion‑hand’) ...
sentences.s228-s15: They departed; therefore the Kandavaran, Buzhar (the water‑given), and the bird‑bringers.
sentences.s228-s16: When Shangal fled from Pilten, that assembly was scattered.
sentences.s228-s37: for there is no one inside who can fight him; there is no army in the world that can be arrayed against him.
sentences.s228-s38: Whoever treats these words as a mere tale, no wise person will agree with him.
sentences.s228-s39: That the famed Perkhashkhar of a hundred thousand would not have been sufficient against a single horseman.
sentences.s228-s40: From this grudge ill befell Afrasiab; because of Rostam, where can he find peace and sleep?
sentences.s228-s21: Let no one fight the dragon alone; if he seeks the fight, he will not find release.
sentences.s228-s22: The khaghan said to him: 'At dawn your counsel was one thing and your thought another.'
sentences.s228-s23: He ordered the army that, all together, they should drive (it) off the mountain.
sentences.s228-s24: They brought the honored one into the midst; they dealt life‑harm to the stout man.
sentences.s228-s5: Thus Rostam said that from the Creator I sought nothing but this clear wish.
sentences.s228-s6: that a stranger from that great assembly would show daring and seek combat with me.
sentences.s228-s7: None of these will remain among them: neither s‌قلاب nor India, nor Indian swords nor Chinese birds.
sentences.s228-s8: I will leave none of their root or foundation in place; I will not leave head, hand, or foot to the Turks.
sentences.s228-s33: Because of so many spears, maces, Gopāl and swords, you said that drops (dew) keep falling from the blades.
sentences.s228-s34: The battlefield plain was full of the slain: bodies and backs and heads, and rags and helmets.
sentences.s228-s35: From the Chinese and Shagani and from the Indians, from Saklāb and Harī and from Pahlav (Parthia).
sentences.s228-s36: The host was like dust at the foot of the mountain, (all) made into one company by a single man.
sentences.s228-s45: I will leave not one of them — neither Shaghnān nor Saqlāb nor Chin — that will set its foot upon the ground.
sentences.s228-s46: For today is the day of our victory; the lofty heaven brightens our host.
sentences.s228-s47: If now the Just One grants strength, he will bring forth Rakhsh, the radiant in prowess.
sentences.s228-s48: On this plain I will make a cemetery; I will make Barūmand into a townsman (i.e. establish him in a city).
sentences.s228-s53: Fear not their multitude; from that sea they lifted up dust.
sentences.s228-s54: All eyes were fixed upon my helmet when I raised my battle-cry and they shouted.
sentences.s228-s55: They tore apart the ranks of Soghlāb and Chin; the earth could not see the sky.
sentences.s228-s56: From that place advanced, like a drunken elephant, one who bore an ox‑bodied mace in his hand.
sentences.s228-s49: One of you, go toward the army; strive, and be a match for the wind.
sentences.s228-s50: Strike when I stir from my place; you raise high the cymbals and drums.
sentences.s228-s51: Make the earth entirely ebony; ride about, horsemen, and sound the war-drum.
sentences.s228-s52: Strike — Gopala and the heavy mace — like the blacksmiths' sledgehammer on steel.
sentences.s228-s1: The shangl roared in front of the host; "It is I," said Gerdaujan the warrior.
sentences.s228-s2: Tell (me) where that Sagzi man is; I will send someone to go with a spear held straight.
sentences.s228-s3: When the cry of the shangl reached Rostam, he looked out from among the host and saw him.
sentences.s228-s4: He said to him, “Behold, I have come, O warrior; watch that you do not take refuge with the host.”
sentences.s228-s17: He killed two of them with his sword; the brave Turanian warriors turned their backs.
sentences.s228-s18: Shangal leapt out of Rostam's hand; he had armor, and first the cuirass covered his body.
sentences.s228-s19: Shangal said thus: 'This man is like no other; no one in the world equals him.'
sentences.s228-s20: A single ragged elephant stood on the mountain's ridge; unless the whole troop at once prepares for battle.
sentences.s228-s25: At that time the lion took up his sword and broke the left hand (left flank) of the Chinese army.
sentences.s228-s26: Whenever you drew your dagger, you threw every body on the road down headless.
sentences.s228-s27: No mountain could keep its footing by his fighting, nor could an elephant hold its place by his anger.
sentences.s228-s28: They gathered about him in such a way that the sun grew dark because of him.
